finding_text: BeigeSage (fine-tuned RoBERTa) achieved the highest overall classification performance on Beige Book sentiment: accuracy=0.71, macro F1=0.71, MCC=0.55 on a 200-chunk held-out test set across three classes (positive, negative, mixed).
method_used: Hold-out test of 200 human-labelled Beige Book chunks; macro F1 and multi-class MCC.
model_specification: Fine-tuned RoBERTa: further pre-training on 1970-2023 Beige Book corpus (~6.8M words), then supervised fine-tuning on 800 labelled chunks for 3 epochs, batch size 4.

finding_text: Llama 3.1 (8B parameters, zero-shot) achieved accuracy=0.68, F1=0.67, MCC=0.52 on the same Beige Book test set, the best among non-fine-tuned LLMs.
method_used: Zero-shot prompting, hold-out test of 200 chunks.
model_specification: Llama 3.1 8B run locally, zero-shot prompt asking for label as JSON value.

finding_text: GPT-4o (zero-shot, OpenAI API) achieved accuracy=0.63, F1=0.62, MCC=0.48 on the Beige Book test set, underperforming both BeigeSage and Llama 3.1 8B despite being a much larger closed-source model.
method_used: Zero-shot via OpenAI API, hold-out test of 200 chunks.

finding_text: Phi3 (3.8B parameters, Microsoft, zero-shot) achieved accuracy=0.62, F1=0.60, MCC=0.45, demonstrating that small open-source models can approach larger closed-source LLMs on Beige Book sentiment.
method_used: Zero-shot prompting, hold-out test of 200 chunks.

finding_text: Gemma2 (9B parameters, Google, zero-shot) achieved accuracy=0.59, F1=0.55, MCC=0.41, the weakest of the LLMs tested.
method_used: Zero-shot prompting, hold-out test of 200 chunks.

finding_text: VADER (lexical baseline) achieved accuracy=0.49, F1=0.43, MCC=0.27, substantially below all LLMs. VADER over-predicted positive sentiment (76% predicted positive vs. 39% human) and under-predicted mixed (8% vs. 42% human).
method_used: Lexical compound score binned to three classes; hold-out test of 200 chunks.
model_specification: VADER (Hutto & Gilbert 2014) compound score with -0.2/0.2 bin thresholds.

finding_text: Non-fine-tuned LLMs systematically over-predict the mixed class on Beige Book chunks: predicted mixed share ranged 65.5% (Llama) to 78.5% (Gemma2), versus a 41.5% human base rate. BeigeSage matched the human base rate at 40.5%.
effect_size: Mixed-class overprediction +24 to +37 percentage points for non-fine-tuned LLMs; -1 pp for BeigeSage.
method_used: Class-distribution comparison between predicted labels and human labels on 200-chunk test set.
model_specification: Frequency comparison of predicted vs. human three-class label distributions.

finding_text: BeigeSage achieved much higher recall on the positive class (82%) than non-fine-tuned LLMs (29%-47%), while maintaining the highest negative-class recall (64%). Non-fine-tuned LLMs achieved very high mixed-class recall (90%-95%) only at the cost of large recall losses on positive and negative classes.
effect_size: BeigeSage positive recall=0.82 vs. 0.29-0.47 for non-fine-tuned LLMs; negative recall=0.64 vs. 0.41-0.62.
model_specification: Per-class recall computed on confusion matrix.

finding_text: BeigeSage sentiment is positively correlated with monthly real GDP growth: r=0.29, p<0.001 (N=470 Beige-Book-period observations).
method_used: Pearson correlation between normalized BeigeSage sentiment (-1 to 1) and Brave-Butters-Kelley monthly GDP growth-rate estimate.
model_specification: Pearson r on contemporaneous BeigeSage sentiment and BBK GDP growth.

finding_text: BeigeSage sentiment is negatively correlated with the U.S. unemployment rate: r=-0.24, p<0.001 (N=470).
model_specification: Pearson r on contemporaneous BeigeSage sentiment and BLS U-3.

finding_text: BeigeSage sentiment is positively correlated with the Consumer Price Index: r=0.42, p<0.001 (N=470). Higher Beige Book sentiment co-moves with higher inflation, consistent with strong demand environments generating both optimism and price pressure.
model_specification: Pearson r on contemporaneous BeigeSage sentiment and BLS CPI.

finding_text: BeigeSage sentiment provides incremental forecasting power for GDP growth at 2-3 Beige-Book-period horizons but adds little at 1 period: ΔR² = 0.003 at h=1, 0.022 at h=2, 0.032 at h=3, on top of a baseline AR(3) model whose own R² collapses from 0.451 (h=1) to 0.043 (h=2) and 0.040 (h=3). For unemployment and CPI, sentiment adds smaller increments and the AR baseline already explains most variance.
effect_size: ΔR² up to 0.035 (unemployment, h=3); GDP ΔR² 0.022-0.032 at h=2-3 horizons.
method_used: Autoregressive forecasting models with three Beige-Book-period lags of the dependent variable plus contemporaneous BeigeSage sentiment as predictor.
model_specification: y_t = alpha + sum_{l=1..3} beta_l * y_{t-l} + gamma * sentiment_t + e_t, estimated separately for h=1, 2, 3 forecast horizons.
